metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC6's CI-wiring proof to also assert `--check` is the invocation mode, e.g.: `for f in ...; do grep -qF 'gen_mcp_reference.py' "$f" && grep -qF -- '--check' "$f" || echo "NOT WIRED OR MISSING --check: $f"; done`
findings.finding: AC6's CI-wiring proof command (`grep -qF 'gen_mcp_reference.py' "$f"`) only checks that the script *name* appears in the workflow file, not that it is invoked with `--check`. An implementer who adds `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (generate mode, no flag) to CI would satisfy AC6 but the drift gate would silently do nothing — it would regenerate the file and always exit 0.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC6 grep with a two-part check:
```sh
for f in .github/workflows/test.yml .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml; do
  grep -qF 'gen_mcp_reference.py' "$f" && grep -A2 'gen_mcp_reference.py' "$f" | grep -qF -- '--check' \
    || echo "NOT WIRED OR MISSING --check: $f"
done
```
Alternatively, assert the exact invocation string: `grep -qF 'gen_mcp_reference.py --check' "$f"`

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the generator must drive `mcp.list_tools()` via `asyncio.run()` (matching the existing codebase pattern `asyncio.run(mcp.list_tools())`) to obtain the list of `Tool` objects with `.name` and `.description` attributes.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the generator to 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP' and 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)' but never specifies that FastMCP.list_tools() is async and must be driven with asyncio.run(). Every existing codebase caller uses `asyncio.run(mcp.list_tools())` to get a synchronous list of Tool objects; calling `mcp.list_tools()` without await/run yields a coroutine, not a list, so the generator would silently produce zero tool rows.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0; read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'After calling each registrar, enumerate tools via `asyncio.run(mcp.list_tools())` — `list_tools()` is a coroutine; the existing test suite pattern (`asyncio.run(build_server().list_tools())`) is the reference.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a comment block in `gen_mcp_reference.py` and a CLAUDE.md entry explaining the closed special-case table, its four current entries, and the rule for extending it; optionally add an assertion in the generator that warns when a tool appears in a registrar but is classified into a different tier (catching new hybrids proactively).
findings.finding: The closed 4-entry special-case classification table (reconcile, fsck, sign_review, run_workflow) is hardcoded inside gen_mcp_reference.py with no update planned for CLAUDE.md or any design doc. A developer adding a 5th hybrid tool has no discoverable signal that this table exists and must be updated — the drift check only asserts the existing 4 names are still present, so a NEW hybrid is silently misclassified rather than caught.
findings.location: Scope section / Classification rule section / AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In CLAUDE.md, add a section 'MCP reference generator — special-case table' listing the four hybrid tools and the rule: 'If a new hybrid is added, update SPECIAL_CASES in scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py and this table.' In the generator, add a runtime assertion that every key in SPECIAL_CASES is actually found in the expected registrar during generation, and emit a warning (not failure) if a tool's readOnlyHint/destructiveHint pattern suggests it might be a hybrid but it's not in the table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit README.md and CLAUDE.md for any existing MCP tool listings or doc-location pointers; either remove/redirect them to `docs/mcp-reference.md` or confirm none exist (add a note to the plan either way).
findings.finding: The plan does not audit whether existing documentation (README.md, CLAUDE.md, or any current partial MCP tool listing) contains MCP tool references that will become stale or conflict with the new generated docs/mcp-reference.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the `_FakeMcp` minimal-stub (and its companion `ctx = SimpleNamespace(...)`) into a shared pytest fixture (e.g. `tests/unit/conftest.py` or a new `tests/unit/fixtures/mcp_stubs.py`) now that three test files need it (Rule-of-Three met), rather than inlining a third copy in `test_gen_mcp_reference.py`.
findings.finding: The planned test_gen_mcp_reference.py will create a third standalone inline copy of the _FakeMcp minimal-stub pattern that already appears without sharing in test_plan_review_explain.py:19 and test_read_freshness_parity.py:165. Rule-of-Three is now met, making this the right moment to extract the stub to a shared test fixture rather than accumulating a third divergent copy.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_gen_mcp_reference.py (new) — Files section; compare tests/unit/test_plan_review_explain.py:19 and tests/unit/test_read_freshness_parity.py:165
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `fake_mcp_ctx` fixture to `tests/unit/conftest.py` returning `(_FakeMcp(), SimpleNamespace(readonly=lambda: False, allow_llm=lambda: False, allow_jira_sync=lambda: False, cap_workflow_payload=lambda *a,**k: None, dump=lambda *a,**k: None, MODE_CAPS={}, Mode=None, logger=logging.getLogger('test')))` and have all three test files import it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the Files edit list and include an entry for `docs/mcp-reference.md` in the appropriate audience section (Agent and/or Operator) of the docs index.
findings.finding: docs/README.md is an unenumerated consumer that the plan does not list as an edit target. It is a curated, audience-grouped index of every docs/ file; it explicitly carries a dedicated Operator-section entry for docs/env-vars.md (the direct parallel to docs/mcp-reference.md). Adding docs/mcp-reference.md without updating README.md leaves the index stale — the new generated reference becomes undiscoverable to anyone consulting the index.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Operator section of docs/README.md: `- **[mcp-reference.md](mcp-reference.md)** — the generated reference of all 47 MCP tools classified by gate tier (always/LLM-gated/write-gated), kept in sync by a CI drift gate.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` to the Files edit list and add an entry for the new MCP-reference drift gate to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` (e.g., `'mcp-reference drift gate': 'scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py'`).
findings.finding: The plan's Files section does not include test_ci_workflow_parity.py as a file requiring an edit, but the `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` dict in that file is a hand-maintained, explicitly-named registry of every gating check signature. The plan relies on the auto-script asymmetry test (test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci) for correctness, but _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES (tested by test_verified_gate_runs_every_shared_gate) lists every other generator gate by its command signature ('env-var registry drift gate': 'scripts/gen_env_registry.py', 'server.json env-contract drift gate': 'scripts/check_server_manifest.py', etc.). The plan makes no mention of adding a corresponding entry for the new gen_mcp_reference.py gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to _SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES in test_ci_workflow_parity.py: `"mcp-reference drift gate": "scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py",`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden the AC4 count regex to include digits in tool names, e.g. `grep -cE '^\| ?`?[a-z_0-9]+`? '` (add `0-9` to the character class).
findings.finding: The AC4 count-proving regex `grep -cE '^\| ?`?[a-z_]+`? '` restricts tool names to `[a-z_]` (no digits). If any MCP tool name contains a digit (e.g. a future `run_v2_workflow` or similar), those rows would be silently excluded from the count, giving a false-low result that passes the `≥ 47` threshold only by coincidence.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the regex from `[a-z_]+` to `[a-z_0-9]+`: `grep -cE '^\| ?`?[a-z_0-9]+`? ' docs/mcp-reference.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `gen_mcp_reference.py` fails fast (non-zero exit + clear stderr message) if any registrar enumeration yields fewer tools than the expected floor (e.g., assert reads≥23, llm≥6, writes≥18 before writing output), and that the output file is written atomically (temp file + rename) so a partial write cannot persist.
findings.finding: The generator script has no specified error handling when registrar import or FastMCP instantiation fails. If a registrar module raises on import (e.g., missing optional dependency, import-time side effect), the script will either crash with an unhandled traceback or silently produce a partial/empty reference with fewer than 47 tools. The count-check AC would catch this only if run immediately after generation; a stale partial file committed earlier would pass --check if the partial output happens to be reproducible.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'After enumerating all three registrars, assert len(read_tools)>=23, len(llm_tools)>=6, len(write_tools)>=18; raise SystemExit with a descriptive message if any floor is violated. Write output to a .tmp file and os.replace() it into place atomically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `--check` mode handles the case where `docs/mcp-reference.md` does not exist by exiting non-zero with an explicit message directing the user to run the generator (rather than raising an unhandled exception).
findings.finding: The --check mode's behaviour when docs/mcp-reference.md does not yet exist (first-run scenario or after a clean checkout that omits the generated file) is unspecified. A developer who clones the repo and runs --check before ever running the generator will get an unhandled FileNotFoundError or silent diff-tool failure rather than a clear actionable message ('file not found — run gen_mcp_reference.py first').
findings.location: Scope section — '--check drift mode'; AC5 drift-gate verification
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'In --check mode, if docs/mcp-reference.md is absent, print "docs/mcp-reference.md not found — run gen_mcp_reference.py to generate it" and exit 1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact FastMCP synchronous API for enumerating registered tools and accessing their descriptions (e.g., confirm `mcp._tool_manager.tools` exists, is synchronously accessible, and carries a `.description` attribute in the installed version) before committing to that introspection path, OR pivot to extracting descriptions from `tool_func.__doc__` (the decorated function's docstring), which is library-version-independent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the generator will introspect registered FastMCP tools via `.description` on each tool object, but the specific synchronous FastMCP internal API for enumerating registered tools after `register_*(mcp, ctx)` is called is never verified against the installed library version. FastMCP's `list_tools()` is an async coroutine (MCP protocol method); the synchronous internal registry (e.g., `mcp._tool_manager.tools`) is an undocumented implementation detail that could differ across FastMCP releases. If the installed version exposes descriptions differently or only via async paths, the generator's core introspection loop silently breaks — producing empty or malformed output that the drift gate then ossifies.
findings.location: Scope section: 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `.description` introspection on the FastMCP object with `tool_func.__doc__.splitlines()[0]` read directly off each registrar-decorated function — the tool decorator wraps the function but the docstring is preserved on `__wrapped__` or the original. This is stable across FastMCP versions and mirrors how `gen_env_registry.py` reads only stdlib AST. Alternatively, pin the FastMCP version and add a one-line API-probe in the generator's module-level import block that fails with a clear message if the expected attribute is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify FastMCP's programmatic tool-enumeration API (the specific attribute path to access registered tool names and descriptions from a bare FastMCP instance without running the server) against the installed `mcp` library version, and document the confirmed access pattern in the generator's implementation notes.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the generator reads tool descriptions via `.description` on FastMCP-registered tool objects, but FastMCP's internal tool-registry API for programmatic tool enumeration is unverified against the installed library version. The existing generator pattern (gen_env_registry.py) uses AST scanning, not FastMCP introspection — there is no established precedent in the codebase for iterating over a FastMCP instance's registered tools and reading their attributes. If FastMCP exposes tools under a different attribute path (e.g. `mcp._tool_manager.tools`, `mcp.list_tools()` as a coroutine, etc.) the generator will silently produce an empty or broken reference.
findings.location: Scope section: 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Before finalizing the generator design, add a one-line probe to confirm the access pattern: `mcp = FastMCP('probe'); <register one tool>; tools = list(mcp._tool_manager.tools.values()); print(tools[0].description)` (or the correct path) — confirm this works synchronously, then document the path in the generator's module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete implementation step for override-table application in the generator spec: after collecting each registrar's tool names, apply the closed `{tool_name: tier_override}` dict (e.g. `{'sign_review': 'Write-gated', 'run_workflow': 'Write-gated+LLM-annotation'}`) before emitting sections, so the implementer knows the exact routing mechanism.
findings.finding: The classification override protocol for `sign_review` (and other hybrid tools) is described at intent level but the implementation mechanism is never specified: the generator enumerates the LLM registrar and gets a list of tool names, but no specification is given for HOW it then routes `sign_review` to the Write-gated section — whether it hardcodes the name as a lookup key against the closed override table, checks tool annotations (readOnlyHint=False is present but that's not the gate), or some other mechanism. Since there is no per-tool gate attribute in the codebase, the implementer must invent this routing step without guidance.
findings.location: Scope section / Classification rule table — 'LLM | REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM | sign_review -> REBAR_MCP_READONLY (HYBRID: in the LLM registrar but write-gated)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The generator maintains an explicit `OVERRIDE_TIER = {"sign_review": "Write-gated", "run_workflow": "Write-gated"}` dict; after enumerating each registrar, each tool name is looked up in OVERRIDE_TIER and its section assignment is replaced if found. The drift check asserts each override-table name appears in the enumerated tool list of the expected registrar (e.g. sign_review must appear in the LLM registrar\'s raw enumeration before override).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact FastMCP introspection API the generator uses to enumerate tool names and descriptions (e.g., `mcp._tool_manager.tools` dict, or a specific public API), and state whether mcp+pydantic extras are required at generator run time with a CI note on how they are guaranteed to be available.
findings.finding: The mechanism for extracting tool descriptions from the FastMCP server object is unspecified: the plan says 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)' but does not specify the API path — whether the generator calls `list_tools()` on the FastMCP instance (which is an async MCP protocol call), accesses internal FastMCP attributes (e.g. `mcp._tools`), or uses some other introspection. FastMCP's internal structure is not part of any plan-defined schema, and this is a load-bearing implementation detail since the generator must work without a live MCP client.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 (writes register); read each tool's name + first-line summary (from `.description`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact mechanism for ensuring writes register during enumeration (e.g., 'set `os.environ["REBAR_MCP_READONLY"] = "0"` before calling each registrar, relying on the documented env-over-config precedence in `rebar.config.mcp_readonly()`') so the implementer knows which configuration layer to override.
findings.finding: The `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0` injection protocol is specified at intent level but not mechanism level: the actual gate function `_readonly()` calls `rebar.config.mcp_readonly()` which reads from both `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` env var and a `[tool.rebar.mcp]` config file. The plan says 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0' but doesn't specify whether the generator sets `os.environ['REBAR_MCP_READONLY'] = '0'` before calling the registrar, patches the config, or uses a mock ctx. If a pyproject.toml in the repo sets `readonly = true`, the env var alone may not override it depending on config precedence.
findings.location: Source of truth section — 'The generator MUST enumerate each registrar with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 so writes register.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'The generator temporarily sets `os.environ["REBAR_MCP_READONLY"] = "0"` (env wins over pyproject.toml per `rebar.config.mcp_readonly()` precedence) before constructing the FastMCP instances, and restores the original value after enumeration.'

findings.finding: The held-out unit test for AC3 (section-bounded placement assertions for sign_review, reconcile, fsck, run_workflow) is not listed in the `Files:` inventory and has no specified path, forcing the executor to invent one; the parity test (`test_ci_workflow_parity.py`) won't catch a misplaced or missing test file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; `Files:` block at end of plan
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the `Files:` line and add one sentence: 'The test calls `gen_mcp_reference.py` (or equivalent fixture) to ensure `docs/mcp-reference.md` is current before slicing it.'

findings.finding: The held-out unit test mandated by AC3 (section-bounded assertions for sign_review placement, reconcile/fsck/run_workflow annotations) is described in detail in the AC but is absent from the plan's Files list. Without a named file path, this deliverable is effectively untracked in the edit-set and easy to omit during implementation or code review.
findings.location: AC line 3 / Files section at bottom of plan
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list, and confirm AC3's section-bounded assertions are implemented there.

findings.finding: The held-out unit test described in AC 3 ('a held-out unit test asserts, using section-bounded slicing of docs/mcp-reference.md') is a deliverable with no file path and is absent from the Files section at the bottom of the plan.
findings.location: AC 3 ('a held-out unit test asserts') vs. Files section ('scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py (new), docs/mcp-reference.md (new/generated), .github/workflows/test.yml, .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml')
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list at the bottom of the plan.

findings.finding: The section-bounded unit test mandated by AC-3 (asserting sign_review placement, reconcile/fsck/run_workflow annotations) is described in detail but is NOT listed as a deliverable file in the 'Files:' section. An implementer following the file list will not create this test, leaving the correctness of the special-case classification table unverified.
findings.location: Files list at plan bottom vs. AC bullet 3 ('held-out unit test asserts, using section-bounded slicing')
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list and add a brief description: 'Contains section-bounded assertions for sign_review, reconcile, fsck, run_workflow placement per AC-3; does not re-invoke the generator — reads the committed docs/mcp-reference.md and checks hardcoded expected strings.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
